text: I think you have this sort of, you know, there's a concept of crossing the chasm. There's sort of this longer tail of customers that are maybe a bit more established, have already pre-existing solutions or workflows that you're now having to sort of, you know, help them evolve through that change. And for what we do is we recognize that, like, there is, we think of AI as it needs to be the icing on the cake. And what I mean by that is, especially when you think about finance, like when you think about movements of money and the fact you still have to close your books and you have all these sort of processes in place that still need to pass muster, you need to make sure you just do those table state things really, really well in a really reliable way that instills trust and confidence in your product. And I think once you've gotten that trust from the customer, you can then start to prompt them and sort of show them like, hey, there's a better way of doing this, or there's this other approach you could be taking, or here's how you can sort of move your company forward in terms of enabling them to move faster and focus on the things that matter, maybe less on expenses and things on the back end. and that is a bit of an education journey though for us like honestly for last week i was having a conversation with a cfo who they are still in the mindset of they really like say personal liability cards because like that for them just feels like okay these employees will care more about how they're spending if they feel like they are personally liable and they may not get reimbursed by their company brex we're obviously biased but we think that's like relatively antiquated way of thinking and that you actually should be able to with the right controls in place be able to move forward with more of a corporate liability and actually have your employees on a card, which just makes it easier for everyone on the back end. But obviously, if I'm talking to that CFO, I'm like, hey, here's all this AI things you could do automatically, and you would decentralize decision-making autonomy to these agents that you would program and you'd be able to verify. That is so far away from where they currently are in their journey that that would fall on deaf ears. And so I think for us, the way we try to do it is we want to be as on the leading edge as our customers that are very AI-native, for example, like Anthropic is a customer of Brex, what we're beta testing with them is far more down the line of what agents could do from a finance professional perspective than what, you know, if you're a Fortune 500 company on Brex and maybe you're a more established business and you're maybe sort of a little slower to adopt. So really just tailoring it for the audience. That's good. I want to give a little plug to Brex. So we've been a customer at SeatGeek for a few years. I think with the I say the prior you know pre versions of the product I think we were able you know we got a case study on the site where we achieved like 99 GL account coding accuracy just with the I think we built through like largely deterministic workflows, you know, elegant structuring of kind of budgets and sub-budgets and, you know, intake flows, like routing things to the correct place. And I think one thing that AI has done since, you know, soon after we launched the product is kind of bring that knowledge and know-how to the organization, just automatically configure. and it's just there and it just works and gets to that 99% instead of having to be just an expert that knows everything about every end of the product. Hopefully. Yeah, definitely. Yeah, and then I want to go back to you, Martin, and just talk a bit more about the kind of culture around learning and development and kind of let's say you've got a new hire coming in who may be coming from a larger company where they don't have this kind of robust environment for learning and launching apps. What do you kind of do structurally and organizationally to encourage this? Is it like you mentioned the demo days? Is it these time-off hackathons? Like what's the special sauce? Yeah, for us, it even starts in the interviewing process. So we found interviews, this is not going to be particularly revolutionary, to be relatively low signal. Some people are good at interviewing and terrible employees and vice versa. So we always have like a case study as part of an interview. And I think in the past, they used to be like a modeling exercise in Excel or like you have to create some slides. And we're increasingly asking people to experiment using something in v0 to actually build an application of sorts as part of their interviewing cycle. And most people have this their first time using v0. So you can get a sense for like, are they able, willing to sort of experiment? And you don't need to know any coding. is just, you know, you kind of do some trial and error and figure out how to do some good prompting. But it really starts with that. So even to get hired, you must sort of be able to demonstrate that you can use some of these tools. And then after you start, I think we're, you know, even with, like, channels, there's also, like, a kudos channel where you sort of give credit to people. And, you know, we have a monthly team meeting where we try to have people showcase some stuff that they built We very big on automation very big on experimentation Like they even talk about this internally like we want people to get sort of speeding tickets not parking tickets Move really really fast push boundaries experiment and try new stuff and you're not gonna get in trouble if something like doesn't quite work. You will get in trouble if you don't experiment or innovate. We don't wanna have that type of a culture. So like, what does that mean from a culture perspective? Like we're not afraid. I think it's like a very big one. There's no, oh, I like tried something and it didn't work. It's almost like a failed founder. That's like a status symbol almost that you've sort of been out there and try to build something and you're going to try again to build something else. Yeah. I think to jump onto what Martin's saying, I do think that creating an environment where experimentation is encouraged and is able to be done at scale, I think is super important. So we created like a platform, like an agent platform that any employee can go to and in a risk you know relatively controlled way they can you know build agents there's prompt engineering multi-modal testing evaluation frameworks and they can really just like start to play around with the data that brex has or like what we're doing and know it's in like a contained way that they're not worried about sort of oh you've signed up to some ai platform or tool externally um and then you know brex is either brex or our customer data is just like out into the wild and so i think having a sort of almost a sandbox environment, which this essentially is, to enable employees just to geek out and spend time just iterating on problems they're facing. And they can do it in a relatively unstructured way, I think has been really helpful. And definitely on the hiring side as well, I think. We're almost going so far as to structuring our interviews that there are certain elements you can't do if you don't use AI. And it's not that you have to be AI fluent and be really progressed in your journey and familiarity with these different tools and platforms. but more like there's a certain degree of intellectual curiosity you need to be able to demonstrate and in those sort of hiring frameworks show that you're actually capable of thinking about, okay, how can I do from a blank sort of sheet of paper essentially, how can I get to an end outcome? Yeah, and just tell me a bit more about the adoption. You described this platform, the agents and building, but let's just say within finance, it's like what percentage of your org is like actually, it's just like an 80-20 rule where, you know, a small number of people are like doing most of the development and people are kind of getting the benefits of that Like how do you teach people in a more kind of structured way to take advantage of this Yeah, I think finance actually, ironically, even though we're sort of a company sort of aimed at bringing AI to finance professionals, we were actually relatively slow to adopt because I think for a lot of people, it was just like getting started was like the inertia we had to overcome. I think if you're in a more technical field, so engineering and others, design, you maybe have more sort of fluency in terms of just like starting with a new tool. I think in finance, it's like maybe just like less part of the job day to day. And so a big part for us was just sort of saying to people like, hey, just go spend time, like use a hackathon, like use a demo day to actually really just play around with something without necessarily having to be a deliverable that you're on some deadline or time pressure to meet so that people could just start to get used to engaging with these platforms and these tools. And so I think for us, it was just around getting people comfortable with starting to like use this other mechanism or tool to get their work done. And then, you know, I would say creativity abounds from there because as they get more familiar about where does, you know, different sort of agentic workflows help or hinder, they can start to know when to deploy it. So the AS606 kind of example is, you know, we actually have a lot of our, most of our sort of agentic workflow from the finance perspective is actually built by i would say like our more entry-level like junior level uh folk because they're the ones that probably get most exposed to tasks or workflows that can be automatable can be improved upon with ai and you know the senior the more senior you go maybe it becomes a little bit more challenging because suddenly you're thinking much more about okay well how do i think about your overall annual plan and how to think about like the three-year direction of your business and what's resource allocation. Like, sure, you can maybe, like, jam with ChatGPT and other sort of tools for that, but it may be harder for you to build sort of an agent that does that on your behalf. It's obviously our business, right? But I think everyone's expected to use these tools. I have someone from, like, my HR team here with me today, because they were curious, and they're also Team One of our customers. They're using vZero. Like, they have a quarterly recruiting review later today, and, like, the dashboards and sort of what we're going to go through is a v zero. So you can like click around and it's effectively like a relative simplistic, I'm not sure how many prompts it actually took, but you can sort of have many applications specifically for this meeting. So as I was reviewing it last night,
